:::{#def-g-computation}

#### G-computation

**G-computation** (also called the **g-formula** or **standardization**)
is a procedure for estimating the causal marginal risk $\pi_a$
(@def-causal-marginal-risk) from observed data
[@robins1986new; see also @hernan2020causal, Chapter 13]:

<!-- Reviewer note (verified, do not re-flag): in Hernán & Robins,
"Causal Inference: What If" (single edition, 2020), Chapter 13 is
"Standardization and the parametric g-formula"; Chapter 14 is
"G-Estimation of Structural Nested Models". The Chapter 13 locator is
correct for g-computation. -->

1. Fit a regression model for $\hat\pi(a, z) = \widehat{\Pr}(Y = 1 \mid A = a, Z = z)$
2. For each observation $i$, predict the risk under exposure level $a$,
   writing $\hat\pi_{a,i} \eqdef \hat\pi(a, z_i)$ for short
3. Average these predicted risks over the observed covariate distribution:

$$\hat\pi_a \eqdef \frac{1}{n}\sumin \hat\pi_{a,i}$$

:::

The following example carries out this procedure on the WCGS data.

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/logistic-regression/_exm-wcgs-causal-marginal-risks.qmd >}}

Under the identification assumptions of @thm-causal-marginal-risk,
this procedure consistently estimates the causal marginal risk:

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/logistic-regression/_thm-g-computation-consistency.qmd >}}

The WCGS estimate $\hat\pi_1$ computed above (@exm-wcgs-causal-marginal-risks)
is a realization of the estimator this theorem covers:
as the sample grows,
that estimate is guaranteed to converge to the causal marginal risk $\pi_1$.
